items.title: Publish a Release build to a self-contained local folder
items.description: Provide a single script that produces a runnable local deployment by publishing the application in Release configuration to a local output folder. The script wraps the publish command, lands the build artefacts and configuration in one directory, and emits a short README describing how to start the app and where its data lives, so a non-developer can stand the product up without a build toolchain.
items.applicability: A publish-local-production script shipped with a generated product to create an on-machine deployment.
items.example: scripts/publish-local-production.ps1 runs dotnet publish -c Release to a chosen folder, then writes a README into that folder covering the run command, the default database location, and how to point at SQL Server.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: make the script idempotent and parameterized on the output path so re-publishing into the same folder cleanly replaces artefacts rather than mixing versions. Test implication: a smoke check should publish and then start the output and confirm the app responds. Honest boundary: a published local folder is a single-machine deployment, not a hosting, clustering, or update-distribution solution; production hosting (a service host, reverse proxy, process supervision) is a separate concern.

items.title: Default to portable SQLite and switch to SQL Server by connection string
items.description: Let the deployment choose its database without code changes. Default to a file-based SQLite store so the product runs with zero external dependencies, but detect a supplied connection string (for example via the ConnectionStrings__Default environment variable) and switch the provider to SQL Server / SQL Express when one is present. The selection is configuration-driven and resolved once at startup.
items.applicability: The DbContext provider configuration and the publish script's documented run modes.
items.example: With no connection string the app opens a local SQLite file; setting ConnectionStrings__Default to a SQL Server instance makes the same build use SQL Server instead, with no recompilation.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: choose the provider from configuration at startup and keep the entity model provider-agnostic so the same code targets both stores; surface which provider was selected in startup logs. Test implication: exercise the model against both providers, since type mappings (dates, decimals, booleans) can differ subtly. Honest boundary: SQLite is excellent for portability and single-user or light loads but is not a substitute for SQL Server under real concurrency; the two are equivalent for this app's schema, not for every workload.

items.title: Use EnsureCreated for the portable store and Database.Migrate for SQL Server
items.description: Match the schema-initialization strategy to the provider. For the portable SQLite store, create the schema directly from the model with EnsureCreated, which is fast and dependency-free for a throwaway or single-version file. For SQL Server, apply the migration history with Database.Migrate so the schema is versioned and can evolve across releases. The startup path selects the appropriate strategy based on the active provider.
items.applicability: The startup database-initialization branch that runs after provider selection.
items.example: On SQLite the app calls context.Database.EnsureCreated(); on SQL Server it calls context.Database.Migrate() so the recorded migrations bring an existing database up to the current schema version.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: never mix the two on one database — EnsureCreated does not write migration history, so a database created that way cannot later be migrated without intervention. Test implication: verify each branch produces a working schema and that the SQL Server branch applies pending migrations. Honest boundary: EnsureCreated trades upgradability for simplicity; it is appropriate for the portable SQLite default but is explicitly not a substitute for migrations where the schema must evolve over time (see the dedicated limitation item).

items.title: Back up a SQLite file by copy and a SQL Server database with native BACKUP/RESTORE
items.description: Provide backup and restore appropriate to each store. For the SQLite default, a backup is a copy of the database file taken while the app is stopped or quiescent, and restore is copying the file back. For SQL Server, use the engine's native BACKUP DATABASE / RESTORE DATABASE through sqlcmd, which is consistent under load and produces a portable backup set. The operations script offers both paths.
items.applicability: A backup-restore-health operations script shipped with the product.
items.example: scripts/backup-restore-health.ps1 copies the SQLite .db file to a timestamped backup and can copy it back to restore; for SQL Server it notes the equivalent sqlcmd BACKUP DATABASE and RESTORE DATABASE commands.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: for the file-copy path ensure the database is not being written during the copy (stop the app or use a quiescent window) or the backup may be inconsistent; for SQL Server rely on the engine's online backup rather than copying data files. Test implication: validate a backup-then-restore round-trip reproduces the same data. Honest boundary: copying a live SQLite file under active writes can corrupt the backup; the simple copy approach assumes a quiescent database, which is acceptable for the portable single-user default but not for a busy multi-user SQL Server, where native backup is required.

items.title: Ship a health-probe script that checks a running deployment
items.description: Include a small script that confirms a deployment is alive by probing it over HTTP. It requests a dedicated health endpoint when one exists (for example /api/health) and otherwise falls back to the root path, treating a prompt successful response as healthy. This gives operators a one-command way to confirm the app is up after publishing, backup, or restore.
items.applicability: The operations script's health mode, used as a post-deploy and post-restore smoke check.
items.example: backup-restore-health.ps1 in health mode issues a request to /api/health or, if absent, to /, and reports healthy when it gets a timely success status.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: keep the probe lightweight and bound its timeout so a hung app is reported as unhealthy rather than hanging the script; do not have the probe perform expensive work. Test implication: assert the probe reports healthy against a running instance and unhealthy when the app is down. Honest boundary: an HTTP liveness probe confirms the process is responding, not that the database is consistent or that all dependencies are functioning; deeper readiness checks are a separate, deliberate addition.

items.title: Write every manual fix back into the generator, never as a one-off product edit
items.description: The core discipline of a reproducible generator is that no change lives only in the emitted product. Every manual engineering decision — a new file, a fixed template, a spec entry, or generator logic — must be written back into the generator's templates, specification, or code so that regenerating reproduces it exactly. A fix applied only to the generated output is a defect against reproducibility, because the next generation will silently undo it.
items.applicability: Any time an engineer hand-edits generated output to add or fix behaviour during a generation sprint.
items.example: Authentication was added by hand (PasswordHasher, UserAuthService, cookie wiring, login UI, seeding), but each piece was committed into the erp-core templates, the spec, and the generator rather than left in the product, so a fresh generation reproduces the entire auth layer identically.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: after any manual product change, mirror it into the generator source and re-emit to confirm the product comes back identical; treat a difference as a bug in the write-back. Test implication: a reproduction run should regenerate from the templates/spec and compare against the hand-built reference. Honest boundary: this discipline guarantees reproducibility only for deterministic template/spec/code output; genuinely non-deterministic steps (see the LLM-module item) cannot be made bit-identical and must be reported separately rather than forced.

items.title: Provide a deterministic generation mode driven by a reference spec
items.description: Expose an explicit, deterministic generation mode that defaults to a fixed reference specification, so a generation run is reproducible by construction. The mode selects a known spec file and emits purely from the templates and that spec, without any non-deterministic step, producing the same output every time it runs on the same generator version.
items.applicability: A generator command-line mode used to produce the canonical, comparable build of the product.
items.example: Invoking the generator with --mode erp-gold defaults the module spec to specs/erp-gold-reference.json; the emitted GoldGenerated output is produced purely deterministically from the same templates as the hand-built reference.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: ensure the deterministic mode has no hidden sources of variation (timestamps, random ids, unordered iteration) baked into emitted files, or reproduction comparisons will show spurious diffs. Test implication: run the deterministic mode twice and assert identical output. Honest boundary: determinism holds for the template/spec-driven portion only; any module that depends on a local-LLM proposal is excluded from this mode's guarantees and must be generated and measured separately.

items.title: Measure reproduction as module and test percentages against a hand-built reference
items.description: Quantify how faithfully the generator reproduces a hand-built reference by comparing the regenerated output to it and reporting two concrete ratios: the fraction of modules reproduced and the fraction of tests reproduced (or passing) relative to the reference. Setting an explicit target (for example at least 80%) turns reproducibility from a vague claim into a measured, defensible result.
items.applicability: The reproduction report produced after a deterministic generation run.
items.example: Against the hand-built reference, the deterministic generation reproduced the full engine, the auth layer, the deployment scripts, and 23 of 23 spec modules identically with 128 tests passing; the headline metrics were 82% module and 93% test reproduction, both above the 80% target.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: define precisely what counts as a reproduced module and a reproduced/passing test, and compute the ratios mechanically so the numbers are repeatable. Test implication: the comparison itself should be scripted so the percentages are regenerated, not hand-tallied. Honest boundary: a percentage is only meaningful against a clearly defined reference and denominator; the non-deterministic modules must be stated as the reason the figure is below 100% rather than hidden, or the metric overstates fidelity.

items.title: Report non-deterministic LLM-proposed modules honestly as the gap to 100%
items.description: Modules whose content comes from a local-LLM proposal are not bit-reproducible, so they legitimately lower exact reproduction below 100%. The honest practice is to identify these modules explicitly, exclude them from the exact-reproduction claim, and report them as the named reason the figure is not perfect — rather than excluding them silently or rounding the gap away.
items.applicability: The reproduction report whenever some generated content originates from a non-deterministic model step.
items.example: Of the catalog, 5 local-LLM-proposed modules are Gold-only and cannot be reproduced deterministically; they are the explicit reason exact module reproduction is 82% rather than 100%, and they are listed by name in the report.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: tag LLM-originated modules at generation time so they can be separated automatically from the deterministic set in every measurement. Test implication: the deterministic-vs-reference comparison should be run with the LLM modules excluded so the deterministic figure is clean and the LLM gap is reported alongside it. Honest boundary: this preserves the integrity of the reproduction metric but does not make the LLM modules reproducible; if exact reproduction of those modules is ever required, the model step must be replaced with a deterministic template or a pinned, recorded output.

items.title: Emit stable data-testid hooks so generated UI tests stay reliable across regenerations
items.description: When the generator emits both UI and the tests that drive it, it should also emit stable, semantic test identifiers and have the tests select by them. Because the identifiers are part of the templates, every regeneration produces the same hooks, so the generated end-to-end tests keep working and the UI-to-test contract is itself reproducible rather than incidental.
items.applicability: Generator templates that emit interactive views together with their browser-automation tests.
items.example: The login view template emits data-testid hooks (login-form, username, password, login-submit, login-error) and the emitted Playwright tests select by those values, so a fresh generation yields matching UI and tests with no manual re-wiring.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: define the data-testid values once in the templates so the emitted markup and the emitted tests share a single source and cannot drift apart on regeneration. Test implication: the regenerated tests should pass against the regenerated UI without edits. Honest boundary: stable hooks make the tests robust to styling and copy changes but do not by themselves verify behaviour; the generated assertions still have to check correct outcomes, and selector stability cannot rescue a test whose expectations are wrong.

items.title: Emit run instructions and a README alongside the published product
items.description: A local deployment is only usable if the person receiving it knows how to start it. The publish step should write a README into the output folder describing the exact run command, where the database file lives by default, how to switch to SQL Server via the connection-string variable, and where to find the backup/restore and health scripts. The instructions ship with the artefacts, not separately.
items.example: publish-local-production.ps1 writes a README.txt into the publish folder covering the start command, the default SQLite database path, the ConnectionStrings__Default override for SQL Server, and pointers to the backup-restore-health script.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: generate the README from the same parameters the script used (output path, default database location) so the documentation cannot drift from what was actually published. Test implication: a check should confirm the README exists in the output and names the real run command. Honest boundary: a README communicates how to run the product but does not configure the host environment; prerequisites like an installed runtime or a reachable SQL Server still have to be satisfied by the operator.

items.title: State honestly that EnsureCreated is not migration history and exact reproduction excludes LLM modules
items.description: Two boundaries must be stated plainly so the work is not overclaimed. First, the portable EnsureCreated path creates a schema but records no migration history, so a database created that way cannot be incrementally upgraded the way a migrated SQL Server database can; it is a fresh-create strategy, not an evolution strategy. Second, the exact-reproduction figure covers only the deterministic template/spec output and explicitly excludes the non-deterministic local-LLM modules.
items.applicability: Scoping, release notes, and any claim about portability of the SQLite default or about reproduction completeness.
items.example: A reviewer is told the SQLite deployment can be re-created and copied but not migrated across schema versions, and that the 82% module / 93% test reproduction figures exclude the 5 LLM-only modules by design.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: keep the portable EnsureCreated store for fresh, disposable or single-version use and direct any deployment that must evolve its schema to the SQL Server migration path. Test implication: do not assert that an EnsureCreated database accepts later migrations, and report deterministic reproduction separately from the LLM-module gap. Honest boundary: this item exists to prevent two specific overclaims — that the portable store is upgradable, and that reproduction is total — and must be revised if either property genuinely changes.
